title: Network Interface Card
description: Hardware component that connects a computer to a network, handling physical and data link layer functions.
fullExplanation: The NIC is the bridge between software and the physical network. It has a burned-in MAC address, a PHY chip for signal conversion, a DMA engine for direct memory access, and hardware offloading capabilities. Modern NICs offload TCP checksum calculation, segmentation (TSO), and receive-side scaling (RSS) from the CPU — dramatically improving performance.

components.name: MAC Controller
components.description: Implements Ethernet framing, CRC generation/checking, MAC address filtering

components.name: PHY Chip
components.description: Physical layer — converts digital signals to/from electrical/optical signals on the wire

components.name: DMA Engine
components.description: Direct Memory Access — writes received packets directly to RAM without CPU involvement

components.name: Ring Buffer
components.description: Circular buffer in RAM for TX (transmit) and RX (receive) packet queues

components.name: EEPROM
components.description: Stores MAC address, firmware, and configuration

components.name: PCIe Interface
components.description: Connects NIC to CPU/RAM via PCIe bus for high-speed data transfer

offloading.description: NIC calculates TCP/UDP/IP checksums instead of CPU
NIC splits large TCP buffers into MTU-sized segments
NIC coalesces multiple received segments into one large buffer
Distributes received packets across multiple CPU cores using hash
Single Root I/O Virtualization — NIC presents multiple virtual NICs to VMs

packetReceivePath: MAC → DMA engine (write to RX ring buffer in RAM)
DMA → CPU interrupt (IRQ) or NAPI poll
keyFacts: MAC address burned into EEPROM (can be overridden in software)
DMA allows NIC to write to RAM without CPU — zero-copy networking
NAPI (New API) polling reduces interrupt overhead at high packet rates
SR-IOV allows VMs to directly access NIC hardware
Wake-on-LAN (WoL): NIC can power on computer from network
